findings.title: `Client.send()` re-opens a closed client by resetting `_is_closed = False`
findings.detail: Setting `self._is_closed = False` at the start of `send()` (and identically in `AsyncClient.send()` at line 1329) means that any attempt to use a client after it has been explicitly closed will silently re-mark it as open. This completely defeats the purpose described in issue #871 — preventing requests from being sent after the client is closed. The transport was already shut down, but the flag is flipped back, so `is_closed` will return `False` even though the underlying transport is closed. The fix should be to raise a `RuntimeError` (or similar) when `_is_closed` is `True` at the point `send()` is called, rather than clearing the flag. The flag itself should only be cleared when the client is truly opened (via `__enter__`/`__aenter__`).

findings.title: `AsyncClient.send()` re-opens a closed client by resetting `_is_closed = False`
findings.detail: Same issue as `Client.send()`. Setting `self._is_closed = False` in `AsyncClient.send()` means that calling `.send()` (or any HTTP method) on an already-closed `AsyncClient` silently re-marks it as open instead of raising an error. The transport is already shut down at this point, so the request will likely fail anyway, but the `is_closed` flag will now incorrectly report `False`, and more importantly a new `__del__` warning will fire on the next GC cycle for a client that was already properly closed.

findings.title: `AsyncClient.__del__` warning may cause exceptions during interpreter shutdown
findings.detail: Calling `warnings.warn(f"Unclosed {self!r}. ...")` inside `__del__` can raise exceptions if the interpreter is shutting down (e.g., `self.__repr__` calls into partially-collected objects, or the `warnings` module itself is being torn down). The standard pattern used by asyncio and aiohttp is to use `warnings.warn(..., ResourceWarning, source=self)` and also guard with `sys.is_finalizing()`. Additionally, using `ResourceWarning` (rather than the default `UserWarning`) is the conventional category for unclosed resource warnings in Python — it matches the pattern of built-in socket/file warnings and is separately filterable.

findings.title: Initial state `_is_closed = True` is semantically wrong for a brand-new client
findings.detail: A freshly constructed `Client` or `AsyncClient` has never been opened, so it is not truly "closed" — it is "not yet opened". Starting `_is_closed = True` causes: (1) `Client.close()` (and `AsyncClient.aclose()`) to be no-ops on a fresh client since the guard `if not self.is_closed` is `False`, meaning the transport's `close`/`aclose` is never called. This may be intentional if the transport is also lazily initialised, but it is fragile. (2) The `__del__` warning on `AsyncClient` will correctly not fire for a client that was never used — but only by accident. If the developer's intent is to distinguish "never opened" from "opened and not closed", a third state (e.g. `_is_closed: Optional[bool] = None`) would be cleaner. Consider documenting the semantic choice explicitly.

findings.title: `Client.__del__` calls `self.close()` which silently does nothing for an unclosed client
findings.detail: `Client.__del__` calls `self.close()`, but `close()` guards with `if not self.is_closed`. For a client that was never explicitly closed (e.g. used via `.get()` which sets `_is_closed = False`), this means `close()` *will* be called and transports will be closed. However, unlike `AsyncClient.__del__`, there is no warning emitted. The asymmetry between sync `Client` (no warning on unclosed) and `AsyncClient` (warning on unclosed) seems intentional for sync usage, but users of `Client` who forget to close it also leak resources with no feedback. If a warning is desired for consistency, it should also be added here.

findings.title: Warning test relies on `del` triggering `__del__` immediately, which is CPython-specific
findings.detail: The test `test_that_async_client_caused_warning_when_being_deleted` uses `del async_client` expecting `__del__` to be called immediately and emit a warning. This is only guaranteed in CPython (reference counting). On PyPy or other implementations, `__del__` may not be called until a GC cycle, so `pytest.warns` may not catch it. The test should be made GC-safe (e.g., force a GC cycle or mock `__del__` directly).

findings.title: Proxy close tests now require network-like activity to arm the close guard
findings.detail: The proxy close tests (`test_async_proxy_close`, `test_sync_proxy_close`) were changed to add a `.get()` call and change the proxy scheme from `all://` to `https://` so that requests go out unproxied. This makes the test name misleading — they are no longer purely testing that the proxy client closes without error; they now also send a real HTTP request (to `http://example.com`). More importantly, the original tests verified that calling `aclose()`/`close()` without ever making a request works correctly. That case is now untested.

findings.title: `Client.__exit__` guard `if not self.is_closed` skips transport exit if already closed
findings.detail: If somehow `close()` is called before `__exit__` runs (e.g., the user explicitly called `.close()` inside the `with` block), then `__exit__` will skip calling `self._transport.__exit__()`. This might cause the transport to not get a chance to do its cleanup via the context-manager protocol, since `close()` calls `.close()` not `.__exit__()`. This was also a pre-existing potential issue, but the new guard makes it more likely to be triggered silently.

findings.title: `test_that_send_cause_client_to_be_not_closed` makes a real HTTP request without a mock transport
findings.detail: `client.get("http://example.com")` in the sync test will attempt a real network connection unless there's a default mock transport in the test environment. The async version has the same issue. If tests run in a no-network environment (CI), these tests will fail with a connection error rather than asserting the `is_closed` state. The test should use a mock transport or the `HTTPX_TEST_TRANSPORT` fixture if available.

findings.title: Docstring grammar: "Check if the client being closed"
findings.detail: The docstring says "Check if the client being closed" — it should read "Check if the client is closed" or "Whether the client has been closed".
